article_title: Sony Bans Brazil's Afil Games From the PlayStation Store as Its 2026 Shovelware Purge Widens to Whole Publishers
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Sony Bans Brazil's Afil Games From the PlayStation Store as Its 2026 Shovelware Purge Widens to Whole Publishers
content_preview.summary: Sony has cut off shovelware publisher Afil Games and tightened PlayStation Store publishing rules, extending a year of store purges from individual titles to entire catalogs.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Sony has ended its publishing relationship with Afil Games, a Brazil-based studio behind a large catalog of low-effort PlayStation titles, and is removing the publisher's games from the PlayStation Store. According to [Kotaku](https://kotaku.com/afil-games-shovelware-playstation-store-eshop-slop-2000710187), "Afil Games' over 140 titles will be removed from the PlayStation Store," with the announcement surfacing on June 23, 2026. The move marks an escalation in a year-long effort by...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (569 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections. Appropriately careful about attributing each disputed figure to a specific outlet rather than asserting a single authoritative number.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 6 source snapshots read from disk (all status 200, no archive fallback). source-0 Kotaku: confirms verbatim 'Afil Games' over 140 titles will be removed from the PlayStation Store', the Afil statement 'PlayStation has decided not to continue its partnership with Afil Games for future releases' and 'our games will be removed from the PSN Store in the near future', and the June 23, 2026 date. source-1 LEVEL UP: confirms 'the majority of its 140-game catalog has been removed from the PS Store', 'stricter quality and originality requirements', and 'considerably more difficult for publishers to fast-track large quantities of derivative content'; the 'submission process is no longer as permissive as it once was' line appears in the source as reported/paraphrased speech, not as a verbatim quotation (see corrections). source-2 PS LifeStyle (guidelines): confirms 'thousands of games from the PS Store nearly every week' and the 'bloodbath' characterization (source uses 'the recent bloodbath'; article quotes 'a recent bloodbath'). source-3 Outlook Respawn: confirms 'more than 900 of its games pulled from the PlayStation Network', 'around 1,000 low-quality games' (April), January targeting publisher ThiGames, and the 'reduced store visibility, complete delisting, or permanent exile from Sony's partner program' enforcement language. source-4 GamingBible: confirms 'over a thousand games' (January), 'hundreds' delisted, 'limited functionality and AI generated assets', and publishers Welding Byte, GoGame Console Publisher, VRCForge plus title Jesus Simulator. source-5 PS LifeStyle (May): confirms 'quietly nuked another major studio behind dozens of scam games and shovelware on the PS Store, along with all of its games' and the developer name Testagamecreations; the snapshot lists 12 confirmed-removed titles but does not state the number '12' itself (see corrections).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core thesis (Sony ended its Afil Games partnership, is removing the catalog, and tightened PS Store publishing rules) is corroborated by multiple independent outlets. The 'nearly 4,000 games in 2026' figure that previously appeared only in a 403'd source is genuinely absent from the headline, summary, and body; the body instead explicitly notes Sony 'has not confirmed a cumulative figure for 2026'. The Afil catalog-size conflict (~140 per Kotaku/LEVEL UP vs more than 900 per Outlook Respawn) is correctly attributed per-outlet and flagged as unsettled in 'What We Don't Know' — not conflated. Per-wave purge numbers are each attributed to a single outlet. Two minor specifics need a public correction (see corrections file).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. The 'bloodbath' / 'quietly nuked' phrasings are quoted from sources, not the article's own voice.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of the Sony shovelware purge or Afil Games (the only nearby gaming article, the Bungie/Destiny 2 piece, is unrelated).
concerns: The body states Sony 'removed 12 titles from a developer called Testagamecreations'. The PlayStation LifeStyle snapshot (source-5) enumerates 12 confirmed-removed titles but never states the figure '12'; the count is the contributor's own tally of the bullet list (and one entry additionally mentions a separate VR version). Recoverable via a corrections note.

The line 'the submission process is no longer as permissive as it once was' is presented as a direct quotation from an Afil representative, but LEVEL UP (source-1) renders it as reported/paraphrased speech ('noting that the submission process is no longer as permissive as it once was'), not as words inside quotation marks. Recoverable via a corrections note.
recommendations: Five of six sources (levelup.com, playstationlifestyle.net x2, respawn.outlookindia.com, gamingbible.com) are not on config/source_allowlist.txt. Non-blocking; the maintainer may consider adding the established gaming outlets to the allowlist. Allowlist not edited as part of this review.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively accurate, well-sourced, and original. Two minor recoverable issues (an inferred title count and a quotation rendered from paraphrased source speech) are honestly summarizable in a corrections record, so APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS rather than REJECT. The headline, summary, and lead are all soundly sourced and contain no fabricated figures.
